full_response:
Let k ≥ 2 be the library limit.  
For every positive integer t build the following input sequence Σt of
n = k + 2t + 1 readings   

  Σt  =  ⟨ 1 , 2 , … , k ,               k+1 , k ,  k+1 , k , … , k+1 , k ⟩  
                                │──────── 2t terms ───────│

In words: first request the k distinct books 1,…,k once, then alternate
k+1 and k exactly t times.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Cam’s (MRU‐evict) cost
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• The first k requests are all misses  ⇒ k trips.  
• Request k+1 when the cache is full.  
  Cam evicts the most recently checked book k  ⇒ +1 trip.

We now show that every one of the remaining 2t−1 requests is also a
miss, i.e. Cam goes to the library each time.

Inductive invariant just before each pair (k+1 , k)

 (I) The cache contains the k−1 never–again–used books 1,…,k−1 and
       exactly one of the two books {k , k+1};  
 (II) That single surviving book is *not* the most recently checked one.

Base case (before the first k+1): after requests 1,…,k the invariant is
clearly true with the survivor = k (and MRU = k).

• Request k+1: miss, Cam evicts MRU k.  
  Afterwards the cache contains 1,…,k−1 and k+1, and k+1 is MRU.
  Invariant holds.

• Request k   (next of the pair): miss, Cam evicts MRU k+1.  
  Afterwards the cache contains 1,…,k−1 and k, and k is MRU.
  Invariant re-established.

Thus by induction every request in the alternating part is a miss.
Hence

 Cam(Σt) = k + 1 + (2t−1) = k + 2t = n  library trips.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Opal’s (optimal) cost
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Opal needs one trip for each distinct book she ever brings into her
checkout set.

• The first k requests: k trips to obtain 1,…,k.  
• The first appearance of k+1: one more trip (evict book 1, say).  
  Her checked-out set is now {2,3,…,k,k+1}.  
• The rest of the sequence refers only to k and k+1; both are already
  in her set, so no further trips are necessary.

Therefore

 Opal(Σt) = k + 1  trips.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Ratio and un-competitiveness
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The cost ratio for sequence Σt is

 Cam(Σt) / Opal(Σt) = (k + 2t) / (k + 1).

Let t → ∞ (equivalently n → ∞).  
The numerator grows without bound, the denominator is the constant
k + 1, so

 Cam(Σt) / Opal(Σt)  →  ∞.

Thus for every cache size k ≥ 2 there exist arbitrarily long input
sequences on which Cam’s “evict the most recently checked‐out book”
strategy is an arbitrarily large factor worse than the optimal
strategy.  Hence Cam’s algorithm is **not competitive**.

\boxed{%
For every k \ge 2 one can construct sequences for which Cam makes
\Theta(n) library trips while Opal makes only O(1), so the performance
ratio goes to \infty; therefore Cam’s strategy is not competitive.}

Confidence: 0.82
